title: Investigating Knowledge Graphs for Context-Aware Search in Financial Transactional AI Agents
authors: Nurkanat Baisenkul
publish: 2026 18th International Conference on Electronics, Computer, and Computation (ICECCO)
abstract: The rapid advancements in artificial intelligence (AI) and large language models (LLMs) have transformed how complex systems process and analyze information. However, in financial transactional contexts, these systems face significant challenges in maintaining contextual awareness, reasoning accurately, and delivering specific insights. This research investigates the integration of knowledge graphs as a foundational component for enhancing context aware search and reasoning in financial AI agents. Knowledge graphs offer structured, interconnected representations of data, allowing the agents to understand relationships, find relevant information, and provide explainable outputs. By leveraging logical reasoning, augmented memory architectures, and retrieval based mechanisms, this study aims to design and evaluate an AI system capable of contextually accurate decision making in financial environments. The proposed research addresses critical issues such as data privacy, system transparency, and bias mitigation while laying a framework of ethical principles for future studies. Through experimentation, this paper will explore how knowledge graphs can optimize search, improve reasoning processes, and enhance the performance of financial agents. The findings aim to contribute to the development of reliable, scalable, and contextually aware AI systems tailored for the financial sector, setting the stage for their broader adoption in real-world applications.

title: Beyond the Random Walk: Hierarchical Structures in Cryptocurrency and Traditional Markets
authors: Nargiza Zhumalieva, Burul Shambetova, Ruslan Isaev
publish: 2026 18th International Conference on Electronics, Computer, and Computation (ICECCO)
abstract: Financial markets are usually modeled as random walks, but real-world data often reveals hierarchical structures beneath the noise. A multi-resolution investigation of fractal organization and nonlinear dynamics in Bitcoin, the S&P 500, and Gold is presented. Utilizing Detrended Fluctuation Analysis and the Brock-Dechert-Scheinkman test integrated with GARCH-filtering, a multifractal hierarchy is identified in Bitcoin, where trend persistence strengthens systematically from daily to monthly horizons. Short-term complexity mainly comes from volatility clustering. However, when we segment waves across multiple resolutions, a hierarchical price geometry emerges that holds across three orders of magnitude. All analyzed assets exhibit robust positive amplitude-duration scaling, with structural consistency optimized within a 3-7 week characteristic scale. These findings demonstrate that while mature markets approximate informational efficiency at high frequencies, an endogenous behavioral skeleton remains visible through multiscale aggregation. Objective statistical evidence is provided for the existence of recursive market cycles. These results are consistent with the hierarchical premises of behavioral frameworks such as Elliott Wave Theory, though they operate at the threshold of detectable structure and do not provide a direct validation of specific wave-counting rules.

title: AI-Generated Text Detection Methods: A Review
authors: Batyr Sharimbayev, Cemil Turan, Aiken Kazin
publish: 2026 18th International Conference on Electronics, Computer, and Computation (ICECCO)
abstract: With the advent of large language models such as GPT-3.5, GPT-4, and the latest variants, the task of creating text that can pass off as human-written has never been simpler. Although the potential for text creation has never been more exciting, the implications of such technology in terms of authoring, academic honesty, and the spread of misinformation are very serious. The purpose of this paper is to analyze the existing 27 research publications related to the task of identifying AI-written text. Various approaches, datasets, evaluation schemes, and issues associated with the task will also be covered in the paper. The approaches used for text identification can be broadly classified into several main categories, namely the use of transformer networks, stylometry, watermarking, and robust approaches. Although the approaches used for text identification are very effective in the usual environments, there are several issues associated with cross-domain text identification, multilingual text identification, and adversarial attacks.

title: HMAF: Hybrid Modality Attention for Fusing Hand-crafted and Deep-learned Features
authors: Shemonti Barua, Siri Yellu, Sai Chandana Koganti, Jihoon Yun, Sanghoon Lee
publish: 2026 International Conference on Semantic Computing (ICSC)
abstract: Semantic image segmentation has long been studied to identify regions of interest in a digital image semantically. Deep learning-based methods represent a major advancement in this field, achieving state-of-the-art results by automatically learning powerful representations from data. However, these methods often require a large amount of annotated data and struggle with complex scenes or objects that have detailed features. In contrast, traditional hand-crafted feature-based methods are robust to data variations but may lack the ability to capture highlevel semantic information. In this paper, we introduce a new fusion approach called Hybrid Modality Attention-based Fusion (HMAF), which combines the strengths of handcrafted and deeplearned features. Our method uses an innovative attention mechanism to dynamically assign importance to each feature modality, enabling the model to focus on the most relevant information for segmentation. This process involves an attention block that takes both feature sets and produces attention weights, which are then used to merge the features into a unified representation. We validate our approach through extensive experiments on a well-known benchmark dataset, demonstrating that HMAF outperforms individual hand-crafted and deep learning methods and provides competitive results compared to existing advanced fusion techniques.

title: Automated Robust Dynamic Generation of KGQA Benchmark Datasets
authors: Aleksandr Gashkov, Aleksandr Perevalov, Maria Eltsova, Andreas Both
publish: 2026 International Conference on Semantic Computing (ICSC)
abstract: Knowledge Graph Question Answering (KGQA) systems transform natural-language questions into structured SPARQL queries to retrieve information from Knowledge Graphs (KGs). However, existing KGQA benchmarks are static, prone to obsolescence as KGs evolve, and increasingly unreliable for evaluating systems based on Large Language Models (LLMs) due to memorization effects (i.e., a model has reproduced the correct output from existing training data). This paper presents DynBench, a fully automated and robust framework for the dynamic generation of KGQA benchmark datasets. Unlike prior approaches, DynBench is enabled to automatically generate an arbitrary number of new benchmark datasets while preserving the natural-language surface forms and structural complexity of the original SPARQL queries in comparison to the given original KGQA dataset. Using entity and property substitutions within the same KG, DynBench produces semantically consistent question-query pairs and automatically validates them without human intervention. We introduce an automatic validation mechanism that ensures semantic and syntactic integrity through back-transformation and metric-based validation. Two dynamic bench-marks were generated from the well-known datasets QALD-9-plus as well as LC-QuAD 2.0 and evaluated using both human experts and automated metrics to show DynBench’s dataset-agnostic capabilities. Given our results, we can infer that the Levenshtein distance serves as the most reliable automatic validation measure, achieving a precision of up to 0.96 and demonstrating a strong correlation with human assessments. DynBench thus enables scalable, repeatable, and memorization-resistant dataset generation—providing a foundation for sustainable and fair evaluation of LLM-based KGQA systems.

title: Explainable Deep Learning for Legal Judgment Prediction: Based on Structured Data from the Swiss Federal Supreme Court
authors: Liqing Tang
publish: 2026 9th International Symposium on Big Data and Applied Statistics (ISBDAS)
abstract: Legal Judgment Prediction (LJP) has emerged as a pivotal application of artificial intelligence in the judicial domain; however, balancing high prediction accuracy with model interpretability remains a significant challenge. To address this, this paper proposes a novel predictive framework named DBOMLP. By integrating the bio-inspired Dung Beetle Optimizer (DBO) to automatically tune the hyperparameters of the Multilayer Perceptron (MLP), the proposed method effectively overcomes the problem of local optima. Experimental results on the Swiss Federal Supreme Court Dataset demonstrate that DBOMLP outperforms traditional baselines, achieving superior performance across all critical metrics, including Accuracy(0.85204), Precision(0.73365), F1-score(0.60959), Specificity(0.76986), and AUC(0.82580). Furthermore, to demystify the “black box” nature of the model, we employed Local Interpretable Model-agnostic Explanations (LIME). The quantitative analysis reveals that features such as case text length, applicant category, and merged cases are the decisive determinants of the outcome, aligning closely with actual judicial reasoning. This study not only offers a robust technological solution for intelligent judicial assistance but also provides significant insights for the development of Explainable AI (XAI) in law.

title: BiLSTM Neural Network-based Environmental Humidity Prediction for Cigarette Manufacturing Process
authors: Lei Yang, Wenxuan Wang, Tonghao Hu
publish: 2025 International Conference on Artificial Intelligence, Electrical and Electronic Engineering (AI3E)
abstract: To achieve precise humidity control in the cigarette packaging workshop of a tobacco factory and reduce the energy consumption of the air conditioning system, this study developed a humidity prediction model based on a Bidirectional Long Short-Term Memory (BiLSTM) network. The model integrates multi-source sensor data and constructed temporal features, enabling it to effectively capture the complex nonlinear time-varying relationships within the production environment. Experimental results demonstrate that the model achieved a coefficient of determination ($\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$) above 0.94 and a mean absolute error (MAE) below 2% in humidity prediction for the core workshop (K1), meeting the accuracy requirements for typical industrial processes. Performance evaluation across all workshops (K1–K9) confirmed that the model is robust and reliable under most common operating conditions, thereby validating its effectiveness. This research provides a key data interface for transitioning the air conditioning system from passive feedback control to proactive intelligent regulation. It helps unlock energy-saving potential while ensuring production process stability and offers a feasible technical pathway for advancing precision manufacturing and supporting the “Dual Carbon” goals in the tobacco industry.

title: Beyond Accuracy: Assessing the Trustworthiness of Deep Learning Models for Coronary CT Angiography
authors: Kenza Bougrid, Mohammed Ammar, Amel Laidi, Mahmood Alzubaidi, Marco Agus, Mowafa Househ, Mostafa El Habib Daho
publish: IEEE Access
abstract: The use of high-performing deep learning models in clinical settings raises concerns about trust, especially because these models often lack interpretability. In this study, we fine-tuned four different architectures: DenseNet121, InceptionV3, InceptionResNetV2, and ViT-B/16. for the detection of atherosclerosis on coronary CT angiography (CCTA) and jointly evaluated their predictive performance and explainability. Using k-fold cross-validation and held-out test data, DenseNet121 and ViT-B/16 achieved higher accuracy, precision, and recall than the Inception models; ViT-B/16 reached a test accuracy of 96.17%, followed by DenseNet121 with 95.80%, and both significantly outperformed the Inception architectures in statistical comparisons. We applied multiple XAI techniques, including LIME, SHAP, and Integrated Gradients, to characterize how each model arrived at its predictions. DenseNet121 provided localized, vessel-specific saliency focused on clinically relevant coronary segments, whereas ViT-B/16 displayed more holistic, patch-level attention that captured broader vascular context while maintaining strong predictive performance. This combined performance–interpretability analysis advances trustworthy AI for coronary artery disease detection on CCTA by linking automated predictions to clinically meaningful image patterns and supporting future development of explainable decision-support tools in cardiology.

title: Machine Learning Reconstruction of the Intergalactic Medium Baryon Fraction in FRB Cosmology
authors: Jialei Niu
publish: 2026 9th International Symposium on Big Data and Applied Statistics (ISBDAS)
abstract: We propose a machine learning based method for reconstructing baryon fractions in intergalactic media (IGM). The Two Segment Redshift Distribution (TSRD) model is used to generate fake fast radio burst (FRBs) data, and combined with a larger sample size of Pantheon+supernova data, the Gaussian process regression is used to directly construct the proportion of baryon matter in IGM from the data. Our parameter free method significantly reduces reconstruction uncertainty. The experiment shows that our method significantly improves both accuracy and reliability: the reconstruction results are highly consistent with the theoretical model, with less uncertainty. On the other hand, we found that in terms of computational efficiency, neural network (NN) methods are 15 times faster than Gaussian processes (GP). This proves that neural network architecture provides more computationally feasible and scalable solutions in large-scale datasets. This work provides a more reliable tool for FRB cosmology research, which helps to deepen our understanding of the distribution of baryons in the universe, reionization processes, and the problem of “missing baryons”.

title: Research on the Application of Machine Vision Recognition Technology in Damage Identification of Gearbox Components for Rail Vehicles
authors: Xingyu Wang, Zengbin Li, Zining Xue, Mo Chen
publish: 2026 9th International Symposium on Big Data and Applied Statistics (ISBDAS)
abstract: Addressing the issues of low accuracy and poor efficiency in detecting surface damage on Gearbox components of Rail Vehicles, this paper conducts an applied study on surface damage recognition based on the YOLO26 object detection algorithm to enhance product reliability. First, the core total loss function of the algorithm-encompassing classification loss, objectness loss, and regression loss-is thoroughly analyzed. Furthermore, by constructing the CBAM attention optimization model, the intrinsic mechanism of feature map enhancement is elucidated. Subsequently, a machine vision inspection system is established, innovatively integrating the attention mechanism. This enables the model to precisely focus on subtle defects on the component surfaces, thereby significantly strengthening the feature representation of damaged areas. Through experimental verification on hundreds of samples across five key component types, detailed test data were acquired. Finally, comprehensive evaluation based on three metrics-Precision, Recall, and F1-score demonstrated that the overall recognition accuracy exceeded 97%. Experimental results indicate that the proposed algorithm maintains excellent robustness and accuracy even under complex operating conditions, fully satisfying the engineering requirements for automated inspection of rail vehicle gearbox components.

title: Improved Analysis of Push-DIGing for Distributed Convex Optimization over Digraphs
authors: Mingyu Mo, Jiahao Wang, Benqi Liu, Zihan Sheng, Kun Yuan
publish: 2026 9th International Symposium on Big Data and Applied Statistics (ISBDAS)
abstract: This paper investigates the convergence of the PushDIGing algorithm for distributed optimization over digraphs. We improve the convergence analysis of the Push-DIGing algorithm from the nonconvex setting to the convex and Polyak-Lojasiewicz condition over digraphs, with our derived convergence rates explicitly incorporating and quantifying the influence of digraphs. Also, we show that Push-DIGing algorithm achieves a faster convergence rate under Polyak-Lojasiewicz condition than the convergence rate in general convex and nonconvex settings. Numerical experimental results are provided to validate the established theoretical findings.

title: MAC-RAG: A Multi-Agent Multi-Emotional Comment Classification Framework Based on Retrieval-Augmented Generation
authors: Jiatong Wu
publish: 2026 9th International Symposium on Big Data and Applied Statistics (ISBDAS)
abstract: In the era of information explosion on social media, efficient and accurate sentiment classification of comments is crucial for public opinion monitoring and user feedback analysis. Traditional Large Language Models (LLMs) often face challenges such as insufficient context understanding and hallucinations when handling Multi-Emotional Comment Classification tasks, limiting their reliability. We introduces a novel collaborative multi-agent framework MAC-RAG, designed to enhance LLM performance and robustness in this task through Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) technology. The framework decomposes the classification task into two specialized agent processes: a Retrieval Agent and a Reasoning Agent. We constructed a dataset containing 1,000 multi-emotional comments and conducted extensive comparative experiments on Qwen and DeepSeek models. Experimental results demonstrate that incorporating RAG strategies significantly improves classification performance. Specifically, the Qwen model combined with a threshold-filtered optimized retrieval strategy achieved an accuracy of 42.67%, validating the effectiveness of retrieval augmentation in resolving complex sentiment classification problems.

title: An Integrated RF-Optuna-SHAP Framework for Predicting Vertical Displacement of Existing tunnels beneath Induced by Foundation Pit Excavation
authors: Lilan Wang
publish: 2026 9th International Symposium on Big Data and Applied Statistics (ISBDAS)
abstract: The excavation of foundation pits induces significant vertical displacement in Existing tunnels beneath. This study compiles 501 sets of numerical simulation data to establish a Random Forest predictive framework. By integrating the Optuna algorithm for automatic hyperparameter tuning and the SHAP method for model interpretation, the proposed framework facilitates high-precision and interpretable displacement forecasting, relying exclusively on four highly accessible geometric parameters: foundation pit area, embedment depth, vertical clearance between pit and tunnel, and tunnel crossing length. Experimental results demonstrate that the optimized Random Forest model attains an $\mathbf{R}^{\mathbf{2}}$ of 0.9394, an RMSE of 2.5141 mm, and an MAE of 1.9723 mm on the test set. SHAP analysis reveals that tunnel crossing length and the vertical clearance are the decisive predictive factors, providing technical benchmarks for construction planning and monitoring scheme design in foundation pit projects.

title: Research on Insurance Fraud Detection Based on Machine Learning - Solutions under Class Imbalance and Missing Data
authors: Jin Lyu, Yaohao Long, Zhuoran Lyu
publish: 2026 9th International Symposium on Big Data and Applied Statistics (ISBDAS)
abstract: Insurance fraud poses a severe challenge to the global insurance industry, particularly in auto insurance, causing substantial economic losses, disrupting premium fairness, and undermining consumer trust in the insurance system. While machine learning techniques have been widely applied in fraud detection, the coexistence of class imbalance (fraudulent cases $\lt 5 \%$) and missing data ($15-40 \%$ of fields) significantly degrades model performance, and existing research lacks comprehensive solutions that can effectively address their combined impact. This study proposes a synergistic framework integrating dynamic sampling (adapted to real-time imbalance ratios), a three-tier missing data processing strategy (prioritizing critical fields for targeted imputation), and a Stacking ensemble model. Validated on a real Egyptian auto insurance dataset (1,000 records) and the Oracle public dataset (15,420 records) covering diverse claim scenarios, experimental observations show that addressing class imbalance alone yields more significant performance gains ($6-8 \%$ accuracy improvement) than missing data processing ($1-2 \%$ gain). The proposed framework not only enhances fraud detection effectiveness but also maintains good generalization across different datasets, verifying its practical potential in real-world insurance fraud detection scenarios.

title: Profit-Driven Severity Prediction for Automobile Insurance: A Cost-Sensitive XGBoost Framework with Dynamic Thresholding
authors: Zikang Wen
publish: 2026 9th International Symposium on Big Data and Applied Statistics (ISBDAS)
abstract: Accurate prediction of traffic accident severity is paramount for insurance companies in estimating claim reserves and mitigating risks. However, real-world motor insurance data exhibit extreme class imbalance (severe accidents are rare), leading traditional machine learning models to perform poorly in predicting minority-class samples and frequently miss high-payout-risk severe accidents. Here, we propose a comprehensive prediction framework integrating cost-sensitive learning and dynamic threshold optimization. First, we evaluate benchmark models including Logistic Regression, Random Forest, CatBoost, and XGBoost, confirming their inherent limitations in recall performance. Second, we investigate SMOTE-based data resampling methods and find that they disrupt the original data distribution without significantly improving overall performance. In response, we develop an enhanced Cost-Sensitive XGBoost (CSXGBoost) and introduce an insurance-specific financial loss function, using grid search to determine the optimal positive-class weight and classification threshold. Experimental results demonstrate that CS-XGBoost achieves F1-score performance comparable to the state-of-the-art EasyEnsemble algorithm while offering substantial advantages in computational efficiency and model interpretability. By incorporating asymmetric financial costs into the optimization process, our method not only improves the identification rate of severe accidents but also minimizes total financial losses in simulated insurance business scenarios, highlighting its significant practical utility.

authors: Yu Tian, Gerui Lv, Qinghua Wu, Ruili Fang, Yajie Peng, Zhichen Xue, Rui Han, Chuanqing Lin, Xiaofei Pang, Ri Lu, Zhenyu Li

title: Suika: Efficient and High-quality Re-scheduling of 3D-parallelized LLM Training Jobs in Shared Clusters
authors: Yuxuan Wang, Yanbo Wang, Chen Chen, Chunyu Xue, Qizhen Weng, Yin Chen, Zeren Li, Xuqi Zhu, Yongqiang Yang, Quan Chen, Minyi Guo
publish: Proceedings of the 21st European Conference on Computer Systems

title: Robust Voltage Regulation of PV-Rich Distribution System Based on Two-Layer Learning Framework
authors: Xiao Liu, Yongdong Chen, Youbo Liu, Hongjun Gao, Zhiyuan Tang
publish: IEEE Systems Journal
abstract: High PV penetration imposes huge challenges to voltage control in distribution networks. Existing reinforcement learning-based voltage control methods usually require accurate physical models, which are difficult to achieve in practice. To address these challenges, this article proposes a novel two-layer learning framework for realizing model-free voltage control. The upper layer uses deep neural networks (DNN) to surrogate the optimal power flow model. The robustness of DNN is improved by simulating the parameter uncertainty and imperfect observed data. Moreover, to tackle grid topology changes, a transfer learning method is employed to accelerate training DNN models using only minor-incremental data after topology changes. The lower layer utilizes multiagent deep reinforcement learning (MADRL) to schedule PV injected power, aiming to regulate voltage. During the training, upper layer DNN is leveraged to surrogate the original distribution network to provide rewards for agents, which reduces reliance on accurate distribution system parameters. Simulation results on the IEEE 69-bus and 141-bus systems verify the proposed method's superiority, with active power losses reduced by 7.58% and 18.5% respectively; even under network topology reconfiguration, the losses are still lowered by 7.17% and 13.5%, respectively.

title: A Novel Dynamic Switching Based SVM Strategy for Voltage-Level Mis-Jump Suppression in Cascaded H-Bridge Multilevel Converters
authors: Yunhe Wang, Jiawei Wang, Samson Shenglong Yu, Guidong Zhang, Yun Zhang
publish: IEEE Transactions on Power Electronics
abstract: Cascaded H-bridge multilevel converters (CHBMC) are widely used in medium-voltage and high-voltage applications, and space vector modulation (SVM) is one of the most promising modulation strategies for CHBMC. However, in practice, the CHBMC may experience voltage-level mis-jumps, leading to high dv/dt and increased total harmonic distortion (THD). More levels of CHBMC complicate the implementation of the commonly used SVM approach. In response to these challenges, this paper proposes an optimized SVM strategy for mis-jump suppression in CHBMC. It divides the switching process between space vectors into internal and external switching, dynamically adjusting the switching sequence of the space vectors involved in two types of switching actions. The proposed SVM strategy is implemented in the ${\alpha ^{\prime }}{\beta ^{\prime }}$ coordinates, avoiding trigonometric and irrational number calculations, thus reducing the implementation difficulty of SVM in CHBMC. Furthermore, it reduces the common-mode voltage (CMV) without topological modifications of converters or additional data sampling, showing excellent cost-effectiveness. Finally, simulation and experimental results validate the efficacy of the proposed SVM strategy.

title: A Two-Stage Symmetric Image Encryption Scheme Using Mixing and Block Permutation
authors: Riyad Mubarak Abdullah
publish: 2026 18th International Conference on Electronics, Computer, and Computation (ICECCO)
abstract: Secure transmission of digital images over open networks is an important challenge due to increasing risks of unauthorized access and statistical attacks. This paper proposes a two-stage symmetric image encryption algorithm, referred to as SAIC, designed for grayscale images using two secret keys. The encryption process combines mixing, block-based permutation, diffusion, and confusion operations to effectively disrupt the strong correlation among adjacent pixels and to enhance key sensitivity. The security performance of the proposed algorithm is evaluated using histogram analysis, correlation coefficients, Number of Pixel Change Rate (NPCR), Unified Average Change Intensity (UACI), Differential Ratio (DR), and Peak Signal-to-Noise Ratio (PSNR), where PSNR is computed between the original and decrypted images to verify correct reconstruction. Experimental results demonstrate that encrypted images generated with different secret keys exhibit visually indistinguishable Encrypted image, while achieving high sensitivity to key variations. The proposed method attains NPCR values exceeding 99.7% and UACI values above 34% across various test images, indicating strong resistance against differential and statistical attacks. The results confirm that the proposed SAIC algorithm provides effective and reliable image encryption, with potential for further enhancement through increased key size to strengthen resistance against brute-force attacks.

title: Command Pattern Recognition in Linux-Based Cyber Incidents Using Large Language Models for Intelligent Honeypot Systems
authors: Raiymbek Magazov, Fatima Uralova, Gulnur Aksholak, Kuanysh Abeshev
publish: 2026 18th International Conference on Electronics, Computer, and Computation (ICECCO)
abstract: Linux servers exposed via Secure Shell (SSH) remain a major target in command-driven cyber intrusions, yet conventional low- and medium-interaction honeypots often provide unrealistic responses and limited session state, causing attackers to terminate sessions before later attack stages can be observed. This paper proposes an intelligent SSH honeypot based on large language models that maintains context-aware terminal interaction while simultaneously supporting semantic analysis of attacker command streams. The system combines an SSH front-end, an LLM processing layer, centralized logging, and an embedding-based analytics pipeline for clustering commands and mapping them to attack stages such as reconnaissance, exploitation, and persistence. The language model is fine-tuned for Linux command understanding and realistic response generation, and the proposed method is evaluated against token-based and ngram baselines. Results from a six-month distributed deployment with approximately four million interaction events show that the LLM-enhanced approach improves command recognition performance to an F1-score of 0.90 and increases attacker engagement by extending session depth and duration. The findings indicate that LLM-assisted honeypots can provide more realistic deception and richer threatintelligence telemetry from Linux-based cyber incidents.
